steps.title: Identify the sentence structure around the blank
steps.content: Before the blank: “geophysicists like Dr. Estella Atekwana at the University of Delaware rely in part on data about the fault’s physical dimensions and geological [blank]” — a complete independent clause. After the blank: “the Imperial fault in Imperial County, California, the US Geological Survey compiles such data in several ways…” — another complete independent clause introduced by the prepositional phrase “For the Imperial fault.” Two independent clauses need a proper boundary.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘features for’: No punctuation between the two independent clauses creates a run-on sentence. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘features, for’: A comma alone before “for” used as a preposition (not a coordinating conjunction) creates a comma splice; it does not properly separate the two main clauses. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘features. For’: A period ends the first independent clause; “For the Imperial fault” then begins the second sentence as a prepositional phrase, correctly connecting it to the following main clause. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘features and for’: “And” is a coordinating conjunction, but without a comma before it and with “for” following, the result is a run-on that fuses the two clauses without a proper boundary. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
